fields.description:
## Context
Teams using DSO to design features and plan implementations frequently have institutional knowledge documented in Confluence — architectural decisions, API contracts, compliance requirements, domain process guides, and established best-practices references (WCAG, OWASP, USWDS, AWS infrastructure guidance). Today, agents running brainstorm and roadmap sessions operate without access to this knowledge, and implementation plans cite only generic guidance, missing org-specific constraints. The Confluence integration closes this gap: a research sub-agent surfaces relevant Confluence content during feature exploration, while a configurable best-practices reference library injects cited, task-level guidance during implementation planning. This epic provides the URL-based fetch-and-cache infrastructure; it is distinct from epic 0b7c-d811 (embedded static design corpus with BM25 semantic search) and from epic e82e-3888 (live security API client for real-time vulnerability data).

## Success Criteria
- When /dso:brainstorm reaches the end of its Phase 1 understanding loop, a dso:confluence-researcher sub-agent is dispatched with the accumulated feature understanding as context; it derives CQL keywords automatically, searches all accessible Confluence spaces, and returns the top 5 most relevant page summaries ranked by Confluence's native CQL relevance score (configurable via confluence.max_results in dso-config.conf) — without the user providing any URLs.
- When /dso:implementation-plan generates tasks, tasks that match at least one configured topic-tag filter AND pass LLM relevance judgment (a prompted inline call within ref-library-loader.sh using the task description and reference excerpt as input) are annotated with inline citations in the format "> Reference: [Doc Title](url) — [one-sentence excerpt]"; tasks with no relevant references are generated without citations and no error is raised.
- Reference documents (Confluence-authenticated and public URLs) are fetched and stored in /tmp/dso-ref-cache/ with a 1-hour mtime-based TTL; repeated calls within the cache window serve content from disk and emit a "[cache hit: <url>]" message to stderr without making any network request.
- The reference URL list is configurable in dso-config.conf with per-URL topic tags (e.g., confluence.reference.url=https://... / confluence.reference.tags=accessibility,ui); adding a new reference requires only a config change with no modification to plugin code. Tag matching is substring-based against the task title and description.
- When CONFLUENCE_EMAIL or CONFLUENCE_API_TOKEN is not configured, Confluence-authenticated URLs are skipped with a stderr warning and all other workflow steps continue uninterrupted.
- When confluence.roadmap_research_enabled=true in dso-config.conf, /dso:roadmap dispatches dso:confluence-researcher at the start of its exploration phase using the roadmap theme as context; when the flag is absent or false, the step is skipped silently.
- When the CQL search returns zero results — whether due to keyword mismatch, permission restrictions, or misconfiguration — the agent emits "[confluence-research: no results for query: <keywords>]" and continues without failing the session.
- An integration test suite in tests/integration/test-confluence-integration.sh validates using a local HTTP stub fixture (no live Confluence required): (a) the researcher returns at least 1 of top 3 summaries containing a keyword from the seed topic; (b) confluence-fetch.sh serves cached content without network requests and emits "[cache hit: <url>]" to stderr for files written within the last hour; (c) ref-library-loader.sh annotates at least one generated task with a citation in the format "> Reference: [Doc Title](url) — [one-sentence excerpt]" when given a reference fixture matching the task's topic tags.

## Dependencies
None (supersedes original e471-dda3 reference libraries concept). Sequencing note: the dso:confluence-researcher CQL search approach is intentionally pre-MCP; when dso-soyt (Incorporate Semantic Search & MCP) ships, the CQL sub-agent is expected to be adapted into an MCP tool. This epic does not depend on dso-soyt but accepts future migration. Environmental requirement: a Confluence Cloud or Data Center instance accessible from the agent's network context is required for SCs 1, 3, 6, and 7; SCs 2 and 4 function without Confluence when all reference URLs are public.

## Approach
A shared confluence-fetch.sh script handles URL fetching, auth detection (exact domain match against confluence.base_url for Basic Auth via CONFLUENCE_EMAIL+CONFLUENCE_API_TOKEN env vars; curl -L redirect follow for tiny links /wiki/x/<base64> before page ID extraction), and file-based cache management with 1-hour TTL and 0600 permissions. Delivery is structured in two phases within the same sprint: Phase 1 — shared fetch infrastructure + dso:confluence-researcher sub-agent (SCs 1, 3, 5, 6, 7); Phase 2 — ref-library-loader.sh citation injection for implementation-plan (SCs 2, 4). Both phases co-delivered because Phase 2 depends on Phase 1 infrastructure. This epic provides the URL-based reference fetch framework; 0b7c-d811 and e82e-3888 may contribute topic-tagged config entries without owning the citation injection mechanism.

## Scenario Analysis
- Basic Auth credential leakage in curl error output (critical, runtime): curl error output may expose API token in session transcript and CI artifact logs
- Missing confluence.base_url with CONFLUENCE_EMAIL set injects auth into public URLs (critical, configuration): absent base_url with credentials set may send tokens to third-party servers such as w3.org or owasp.org
- Confluence API timeout blocks brainstorm Phase 1 loop (high, runtime): no timeout guard on synchronous API call; session can die mid-brainstorm at the researcher dispatch step
- Concurrent implementation-plan runs corrupt shared cache file (high, runtime): no file locking; concurrent cache writers can produce partial reads fed to the LLM relevance filter
- URL-matching heuristic misclassifies authenticated URLs as public (high, runtime): trailing-slash or case mismatch causes 401 or login-page HTML cached as reference content
- LLM relevance filter invoked on 200+ reference URLs causes token exhaustion (high, runtime): summaries of 40-60 post-tag-filter URLs may exceed sub-agent context window
- Missing credentials silently degrades to public-only mode (high, deployment): empty credentials write error response body to cache with no startup validation warning
- dso:confluence-researcher dispatched in sub-agent context violates nesting prohibition (high, deployment): if brainstorm is itself a sub-agent, dispatch creates forbidden nesting depth triggering tool result missing error
- Self-signed or private CA Confluence instances reject curl TLS verification (high, runtime): on-premises Confluence with internal CA fails with exit 60; no --cacert config support
- CI environment lacks /tmp write access (medium, deployment): no configurable cache directory override for hardened CI runners
- confluence.base_url without path causes Jira URL false-positives (medium, configuration): broad prefix match injects Confluence credentials into Jira API requests
- TTL=0 causes infinite cache bypass (medium, configuration): every call fetches live; 500+ API calls for a 50-task plan triggers Confluence rate limiting

## Research Findings
Prior art: /wiki/rest/api/search?cql=... with Basic Auth (email:API token) is confirmed working in atlassian-api/atlassian-python-api (Python, cql() method with _links.next pagination), aashari/mcp-server-atlassian-confluence (Node.js MCP server exposing CQL to AI systems — direct prior art for dso:confluence-researcher), sooperset/mcp-atlassian (Python MCP server), langchain-ai/langchain ConfluenceLoader (CQL with limit/max_pages/pagination), and coulibal/Confluence-Rest-API (bash curl approach). Hard 50-result CQL cap confirmed (atlassian-python-api issue #1127). Pagination via _links.next is required and well-tested across all these implementations. Tiny link resolution (curl -L redirect follow) is the correct approach per Atlassian community.
